message.content:
The wind holds its breath, a held note in the air,
As twilight bleeds into indigo deep.
The peaks, like jagged teeth, bite at the glare
Of stars that wake from their long, silent sleep.
Below, the valley curls in shadow’s lace,
A quilt of pine and stone, cold and still,
Waiting for night to draw its white embrace,
And silence to bend the world to its will.

Then comes the first flake, a ghost, a speck,
A diamond dusted from the moon’s cold hand.
It drifts, unburdened, slow and weak,
To kiss the roof of the oldest land.
Another follows, then another’s trace,
A whisper woven in the frozen dark,
Turning the sky to a blank, pale face,
Erasing the edge between earth and arc.

The village sleeps beneath a woolen shroud,
Chimneys exhale thin, gray ghosts of wood.
The church bell tolls, but the sound is drowned
By the soft, relentless hush of the flood.
It settles on the cobblestones’ rough skin,
On the iron gate, the frozen stream,
Hiding the scars of the year within,
A dreamless, perfect, crystalline seam.

The maples stand like sentinels in white,
Their branches bowed under the weight of grace.
The world is stripped of its noisy bite,
Replaced by this slow, descending pace.
No footstep breaks the pristine sheet,
No voice disturbs the hallowed air,
Just the rhythm of the falling fleet,
And the quiet knowing that winter is there.

Morning will find a kingdom made of glass,
Reflecting the sun in a million shards.
But now, as the heavy curtains pass,
